--- tags: post layout: "layouts/article.njk" title: Browsers Should Have a Native Control for Per-Site Color Scheme date: "2026-03-25" highlightSyntax: true meta: description: A proposal for browsers to offer a per-site color scheme control. image: /blog/assets/post-image-color-scheme-browser-native-control.jpg ---

Note: This is a proposal for a browser feature. All the demos are mock ups and the feature-specific code is made up. As far as I know, nothing like this is in progress by any browser.

Update: Even though I thought I’d done an extensive search for existing posts on this, my searching didn’t turn up any posts on the topic. But knew there had to be already. And of course, there already is. I swear I know how to Google, I think? Bramus has already written about this, and more. Jim too. You should read those.

Best place to start is a demo. Here’s how this would work in Chrome: The UI shouldn’t be surprising. There’s a color scheme button in the URL bar. Tapping it shows a popover that contains a select. The select options are the site’s available color schemes. Choosing an option sets the color scheme for that site. Just like page zoom, color scheme selection persists for that site. More browser examples and different scenarios below. ## Motivation Right now, every site with multiple color schemes has to implement a custom control in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to let the user change the scheme. Or there’s only code in place to respect the OS-level appearance setting with no in-site control to change it. Both of those feel wrong. Sites with multiple themes, at least light and dark, is widespread enough that it feels standard. But, as a user, having to track down the custom color scheme control on every site is a pain. Sometimes it’s in the header. Sometimes it’s in a sidebar. Sometimes it’s tucked away in a settings menu or page. And some of you psychos put it at the very bottom of an incredibly long page, I think because you’re out to get me. Relying only on the OS-level appearance is less than ideal. Using dark mode at the OS-level, but then light mode for certain websites is common. Which scheme a user wants could depend on the content, the time of day, the quality of the scheme design, or maybe just how their eyes are feeling that day. There’s no one correct setting. **Side note**: Your dark scheme background is too dark and foreground too bright. My retinas are burning. Along with usability, site authors shouldn’t have to reimplement this control for every site they build. They should be able to design their color schemes in CSS and opt in to the native control. ## Prior art This almost exists in a couple places. In Chrome, you can set a browser-level scheme override with `Settings > Appearance > Mode`. You can choose from: Light, Dark, or Device. This overrides the OS-level scheme. But, it’s for the entire Chrome UI and applies to every site. Firefox is even closer. You can set a site-level scheme with `Settings > General > Website appearance`. You can choose from: Automatic, Light, and Dark. This setting doesn’t change the Firefox UI, only sites. But, it applies to every site. Safari doesn’t seem to have anything like this. Zen, which I use, is based on Firefox so it works the same way. I’m not sure about others like Brave, Vivaldi, et al. So Firefox is close, but we need per-site control. ## Opting in The good news here is I think we have everything we need already. Today, we use `color-scheme`, `light-dark`, and `prefers-color-scheme` to create schemes. 
:root { color-scheme: light dark; }

body {
  color: light-dark(black, white);
  background-color: light-dark(white, black);
}

@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
  body { font-size: 95%; }
}
For a site to tell browsers they want to opt-in to showing the color scheme control, I propose we **require** a `color-scheme` meta tag. 
<meta name="color-scheme" content="light dark">
If a site doesn’t have the `color-scheme` meta tag, the color scheme button would not show up. Note that the `color-scheme` meta **isn’t** required for `prefers-color-scheme` or `light-dark()` to work. Using something in the document, as opposed to CSS, feels more solid for signaling to the browser that a site has color schemes and wants to display the control. ## Handling lack of support Not all browsers will implement this at the same time. Some may not at all. Each site, depending on the audience, could decide that’s OK. Users with unsupported browsers won’t have a color scheme control. The OS-level setting will dictate the scheme. That might not work though. Sites should be able to detect support for the native scheme control. This should be a simple check for support in JavaScript: 
if (!navigator.colorScheme) {
  // Setup and display custom color scheme control
}
In those cases, sites can implement a custom in-page control. Not ideal, but ensures all users are able to change the color scheme. Again, `navigator.colorScheme` is something I’m making up, not something real today. The important part is site authors have some way to detect support. ## Implementation This section is a rough overview of how I’d implement this in a browser. **Initialization:** 1. Check for `color-scheme` meta in document 1. Parse `color-scheme` meta `content` 1. Each space-separated item from `content` is an available scheme 1. Display the color scheme button 1. For each available scheme, add an option to the color scheme select 1. If there is a persisted user-selected color scheme, set the button and select to it Browsers only need to set things up. It’s not the responsibility of the browser to ensure valid CSS exists for each scheme. That’s the responsibility of the author. **When user changes scheme:** 1. Set the preferred color scheme for this site to the user selection 1. Persist the preferred color scheme 1. Set the color scheme button icon to one of: system, light, dark, custom I’m hand-waving on #1 because I don’t know how it works today, but I know it works. This should be a similar mechanism to how each site respects the OS-level or browser-level (like the setting in Firefox) color scheme setting. **Preference order** Color scheme can be set in a few different places: OS, browser, site. Those could all be different. The preference is: 1) Site 2) Browser 3) OS. Example: if OS is set to `auto`, browser is set to `dark`, and site is set to `light`, the site will use the light scheme. **Odds and ends** A few things that came up, but I didn’t dive deep into: - Scheme storage by origin: the preference is per-origin, not per-page. Subdomains are separate. - Sync across devices: preference should probably sync via existing browser sync infrastructure (Chrome Sync, Firefox Sync). Though maybe it **shouldn’t** sync between desktop and mobile. - Incognito/private browsing: doesn't read or write per-site scheme preferences. - Reset behavior: Selecting “System” clears the per-site preference entirely, back to the default. Just like setting zoom to 100%. - Dynamic meta tag changes: If JS changes the meta tag without a navigation, the browser should re-read it and update the control. If the stored preference is no longer in the list, fall back to system. ### Precedent When adding a new feature like this, it’s important to find precedent. There’s not an exact match, but there are two examples—in Chrome at least—that have similar ideas. Page zoom and service workers. Page zoom is relevant because it’s a control on the browser UI that changes how each site displays. By using different font sizes and font size units, site authors have some control over how a site behaves when zoomed. Also, in Chrome, Firefox, Zen, and likely others, a button/indicator displays in the URL bar when zoom level is not 100%. Service workers have only a slight relevance. When a site includes a properly configured manifest and service worker, in Chrome, an Install button appears in the URL bar. This is the one example I found where site author code results in a browser UI element being displayed. So, again, the color scheme control proposal isn’t 1:1, but it’s also not completely unheard of. ## Custom schemes Right now, the `color-scheme` property and meta tag only support `light` and `dark`. But the spec is open-ended. The definition includes a `` as a placeholder for custom schemes. That’s only a proposal though, so don’t get too excited. Bramus wrote about this in “[The Future of CSS: Easy Light-Dark Mode Color Switching with `light-dark()`](https://www.bram.us/2023/10/09/the-future-of-css-easy-light-dark-mode-color-switching-with-light-dark/#schemed-value).” There’s no reason this feature can’t follow the same path. Say a site has a custom sepia scheme it wants to make available. The author declares it with the `color-scheme` meta: 
<meta name="color-scheme" content="light dark sepia">
And sets it up in the CSS: 
:root { color-scheme: light dark sepia; }
/* scheme styles go here */
The sepia theme would then be available in the color scheme control: ## More examples In Chrome and Firefox, the scheme button would slot in with any other contextual buttons. Would work the same. Here’s the URL bar of an installable site that’s zoomed in and has available color schemes: Firefox is nearly the same as Chrome, with a scheme popover that includes a select for available schemes. This mock up isn’t as complete, but this is the idea. Zen uses a sidebar for URL and tabs. It also has contextual buttons in the URL bar. That’s where the scheme control would go. I don’t have a mock up of the selection UI, but Zen uses pretty standard popovers and selects. Safari is interesting. It doesn’t really do contextual buttons in the URL bar. It has a reading list and I think reader view, but I didn’t see any others. I don’t see any future where Apple gives up URL bar real estate for this. That means this one would be a bit less discoverable. What it does have is a Site settings button. That opens a popover and one of the items in there is page zoom. Not my favorite, but the scheme control could slot right in there.

This fits nicely with the trajectory and current status of `color-scheme`, `light-dark()`, and related specs. This wouldn’t have been a good idea, say, 2 years ago. We just didn’t know enough yet. But now, feels like we have a solid foundation to build on. I don’t know what the processes are for getting a feature like this in various browsers. I’m sure it’s different for them all. The first step is this, just getting it written down and in front of people. I know I’d use this many times a day every day. Now to see if anyone else would too.

content.text: Good, I've got a solid feel for your voice and post structure. Let me write the draft.

input.content:
--- tags: post layout: "layouts/article.njk" title: "CSS Background Repeat Clamps Display P3 SVG Colors to sRGB" date: "2026-07-13" highlightSyntax: true meta: description: A browser bug where display-p3 colors in SVG background images get clamped to sRGB when background-repeat is used. image: /images/social-summary.png ---

Heads up if you're working with display-p3 colors in SVGs and using them as CSS background images. There's a bug in Chrome and Safari where your wide gamut colors get silently clamped to sRGB. The trigger? background-repeat.

## The setup I have an SVG with a display-p3 red fill: 
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="300" height="300" viewBox="0 0 300 300">
  <rect width="300" height="300" fill="color(display-p3 1 0 0)" />
</svg>
On a P3 display, that red is noticeably more vivid than sRGB red. If you haven't seen the difference side by side, it's not subtle. You know it when you see it. I was using this SVG as a `background-image` with `repeat-x`. The color looked flat. Just regular old sRGB red. I spent a while chasing the wrong things. Layout context, CSS custom properties, WebC scoping. None of that was the problem. ## The bug It comes down to this. If the SVG background doesn't need to repeat, P3 works: 
.works {
  width: 300px;
  height: 300px;
  background: url(/images/my-p3.svg) no-repeat;
}
The moment the container is larger than the image and the background repeats, P3 is gone: 
.broken {
  width: 301px;
  height: 300px;
  background: url(/images/my-p3.svg) repeat;
}
One extra pixel. That's all it takes. As soon as the browser needs to tile the SVG, the color drops to sRGB. This happens with `repeat`, `repeat-x`, and `repeat-y`. It happens in both Chrome and Safari. Firefox doesn't support display-p3 in SVG yet, so it's not testable there. ## Why this happens When a background image doesn't repeat, the browser can render the SVG directly into the final compositing surface. On a P3 display, that surface is wide gamut. Colors go straight through. When the browser needs to tile, it takes a different path. It rasterizes the SVG into an intermediate bitmap first, then stamps that bitmap across the element. That intermediate bitmap is being created as an sRGB buffer. So the P3 red gets clamped the moment it hits the tile, before it ever reaches the screen. ## It's SVG-specific Here's the interesting part. If you use a PNG tagged with a Display P3 ICC profile as your background image, the repeat works fine. P3 colors survive tiling. The browser reads the ICC profile from the raster image and preserves the color space through the tiling pipeline. So the general tiling code handles wide gamut correctly. It's only when rasterizing an SVG into the tile buffer that the color space gets lost. The SVG rasterizer is dumping into sRGB. ## The workaround If you need a repeating background with P3 colors, use a PNG (or WebP) exported with a Display P3 color profile instead of an SVG. Most image editors support this. In Figma, you can set the export color profile to Display P3. Photoshop and Affinity handle it too. It's not ideal if you want the scalability of SVG, but it works. ## Related bugs I haven't found this exact bug reported anywhere. The closest I found: - [WebKit #231062](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=231062) — SVG images drawn onto a display-p3 canvas get flattened to sRGB. Same pattern, different context. - [Chromium #40923665](https://issues.chromium.org/issues/40923665) — WebGL `transferToImageBitmap` forgets the color space. Another case of an intermediate buffer losing wide gamut info. Both are the same class of bug. Intermediate buffers defaulting to sRGB when they should preserve the source color space. If I file a bug for this one, I'll link it here.

content.content:
1→--- 2→tags: post 3→layout: "layouts/article.njk" 4→title: "P3 Color Bug with Repeating SVG Background Images" 5→date: "2026-07-14" 6→highlightSyntax: true 7→meta: 8→ description: Aw geez. There’s a bug in Chrome and Safari where SVGs that use Display P3 colors get changed to sRGB when set as repeating background images. 9→ image: /blog/assets/post-image-p3-svg-bug.jpg 10→--- 11→ 12→

13→ Here’s a fun one. There’s a bug in Chrome and Safari where SVGs that use Display P3 colors get changed to sRGB when set as repeating background images. 14→

15→ 16→

17→ First, a few details: 18→

19→ 20→- Seeing this in Chrome 150 and Safari 26 21→- Firefox (as of 152) needs [silly config changes](https://zidhuss.tech/posts/p3-firefox-mac/) so I didn’t test there. Assuming it has the same issue 22→- This post is about Display P3, but the same applies to other wide gamut colors like oklch 23→- [Demo on CodePen](https://codepen.io/editor/tylergaw/pen/019f5dc8-ea9e-7395-bb62-0f2d8e674ab6) 24→ 25→Keep in mind, you have to be on a display that supports Display P3 colors to see this bug. It’s a bit tricky to show the problem in the CodePen or here because of that. 26→ 27→Take a simple SVG square with a red fill of `color(display-p3 1 0 0)`. 28→ 29→
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="300" height="300" viewBox="0 0 300 300">
    30→  <rect fill="color(display-p3 1 0 0)" width="300" height="300" />
    31→</svg>
32→ 33→ 34→ 35→This works as expected in supported browsers on supported displays as internal SVG and `img`. It shows up as the brightest red available in Display P3 color. 36→ 37→Things bug out when using it as a CSS background image that repeats. `background-repeat` is the culprit here. For example, this works fine: 38→ 39→
.this-works {
    40→  width: 300px;
    41→  height: 300px;
    42→  background: url(/images/p3-test.svg) no-repeat;
    43→}
44→ 45→The Display P3 red square displays as the background of `this-works`. But, if we set the background to repeat **and** set the size of the element where it will repeat, the image converts to sRGB. 46→ 47→
.this-breaks {
    48→  width: 600px;
    49→  height: 300px;
    50→  background: url(/images/p3-test.svg) repeat;
    51→}
52→ 53→This happens with `repeat`, `repeat-x`, and `repeat-y`. Or if you omit the repeat value since `repeat` is the default. 54→ 55→You can fiddle with it in [the CodePen](https://codepen.io/editor/tylergaw/pen/019f5dc8-ea9e-7395-bb62-0f2d8e674ab6). All it takes is for the width or height in the repeated direction to be one pixel more than the size of the image. That’s how we know repeat is the problem. 56→ 57→## Why This Happens 58→ 59→I couldn’t find an existing Chromium or WebKit bug about this, but I found a couple similar ones (listed below). Between those, some reading, and general knowledge of how browsers handle tiling I have an idea of what’s happening. 60→ 61→When a background image doesn’t repeat, browsers render the SVG directly into the final composited surface. So, colors display as expected. 62→ 63→When a background image repeats, browsers need to tile the image. They do so by rasterizing the SVG into an intermediate bitmap first, then “stamping” it across the element as needed. They create that bitmap as an sRGB buffer. That’s the problem. Display P3, or any other non-sRGB color, gets converted (clamped) to what sRGB is able to display. Which causes the bug here. 64→ 65→## This Is Only SVG 66→ 67→An interesting thing is this only happens with SVG. If you use a PNG with a Display P3 profile as the background image, P3 colors survive tiling. 68→ 69→Seems like the root cause is when an SVG is rasterized into the tile buffer. 70→ 71→## A Workaround 72→ 73→I don’t really have a workaround. This is just broken. For my specific use case I think I can get away with using a PNG instead of an SVG. It’s a small enough element that I should be able to use a 2x version and scale it down to maintain crispness. 74→ 75→### Similar bugs 76→ 77→- [WebKit #231062](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=231062) SVG images drawn onto a display-p3 canvas get flattened to sRGB. Same pattern, different context. 78→- [Chromium #40923665](https://issues.chromium.org/issues/40923665) WebGL `transferToImageBitmap` forgets the color space. Another case of an intermediate buffer losing wide gamut info. 79→ 80→

file.content:
--- tags: post layout: "layouts/article.njk" title: "P3 Color Bug with Repeating SVG Background Images" date: "2026-07-14" highlightSyntax: true meta: description: Aw geez. There’s a bug in Chrome and Safari where SVGs that use Display P3 colors get changed to sRGB when set as repeating background images. image: /blog/assets/post-image-p3-svg-bug.jpg ---

Here’s a fun one. There’s a bug in Chrome and Safari where SVGs that use Display P3 colors get changed to sRGB when set as repeating background images.

First, a few details:

- Seeing this in Chrome 150 and Safari 26 - Firefox (as of 152) needs [silly config changes](https://zidhuss.tech/posts/p3-firefox-mac/) so I didn’t test there. Assuming it has the same issue - This post is about Display P3, but the same applies to other wide gamut colors like oklch - [Demo on CodePen](https://codepen.io/editor/tylergaw/pen/019f5dc8-ea9e-7395-bb62-0f2d8e674ab6) Keep in mind, you have to be on a display that supports Display P3 colors to see this bug. It’s a bit tricky to show the problem in the CodePen or here because of that. Take a simple SVG square with a red fill of `color(display-p3 1 0 0)`. 
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="300" height="300" viewBox="0 0 300 300">
  <rect fill="color(display-p3 1 0 0)" width="300" height="300" />
</svg>
This works as expected in supported browsers on supported displays as internal SVG and `img`. It shows up as the brightest red available in Display P3 color. Things bug out when using it as a CSS background image that repeats. `background-repeat` is the culprit here. For example, this works fine: 
.this-works {
  width: 300px;
  height: 300px;
  background: url(/images/p3-test.svg) no-repeat;
}
The Display P3 red square displays as the background of `this-works`. But, if we set the background to repeat **and** set the size of the element where it will repeat, the image converts to sRGB. 
.this-breaks {
  width: 600px;
  height: 300px;
  background: url(/images/p3-test.svg) repeat;
}
This happens with `repeat`, `repeat-x`, and `repeat-y`. Or if you omit the repeat value since `repeat` is the default. You can fiddle with it in [the CodePen](https://codepen.io/editor/tylergaw/pen/019f5dc8-ea9e-7395-bb62-0f2d8e674ab6). All it takes is for the width or height in the repeated direction to be one pixel more than the size of the image. That’s how we know repeat is the problem. ## Why This Happens I couldn’t find an existing Chromium or WebKit bug about this, but I found a couple similar ones (listed below). Between those, some reading, and general knowledge of how browsers handle tiling I have an idea of what’s happening. When a background image doesn’t repeat, browsers render the SVG directly into the final composited surface. So, colors display as expected. When a background image repeats, browsers need to tile the image. They do so by rasterizing the SVG into an intermediate bitmap first, then “stamping” it across the element as needed. They create that bitmap as an sRGB buffer. That’s the problem. Display P3, or any other non-sRGB color, gets converted (clamped) to what sRGB is able to display. Which causes the bug here. ## This Is Only SVG An interesting thing is this only happens with SVG. If you use a PNG with a Display P3 profile as the background image, P3 colors survive tiling. Seems like the root cause is when an SVG is rasterized into the tile buffer. ## A Workaround I don’t really have a workaround. This is just broken. For my specific use case I think I can get away with using a PNG instead of an SVG. It’s a small enough element that I should be able to use a 2x version and scale it down to maintain crispness. ### Similar bugs - [WebKit #231062](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=231062) SVG images drawn onto a display-p3 canvas get flattened to sRGB. Same pattern, different context. - [Chromium #40923665](https://issues.chromium.org/issues/40923665) WebGL `transferToImageBitmap` forgets the color space. Another case of an intermediate buffer losing wide gamut info.

I still need to file Chromium and WebKit bugs for this because all signs point to this being an actual bug. Also I might pull down the Chromium source and see if I can track down the actual line(s) of code at the root. If you’ve bumped into this or have more details, let me know.

When giving copyedit feedback on blog posts, use blockquotes to show the original text, then provide the suggestion below. The user called this out as a nice format.

**Why:** Makes it easy to see exactly what's being referenced and what the change is.
**How to apply:** Use `> "quoted text"` format when pointing out issues in prose, followed by the suggestion.

  height: 300px;

}
The Display P3 red square displays as the background of `this-works`. But, if we set the background to repeat **and** set the size of the element where it will repeat, the image converts to sRGB. 
.this-breaks {

  height: 300px;

}

When a background image repeats, browsers need to tile the image. They do so by rasterizing the SVG into an intermediate bitmap first, then “stamping” it across the element as needed. message.content: Help me think through something. Pull in the latest now-widget.webc. See the style for .inner. That background size. Right now the 100% is what I want, it'll verticall stretch the background image, but the width is hard coded. Is there a way with CSS only that I can make that width be a factor of the height so the image is always kind of stretched vertically?

file.content: • /now

content.text:
So you want the width to be proportional to the height — like "always 80% of the height" or "always 0.8x the height" — so as the element grows taller, the tiles get wider too, staying vertically stretched.

There's no CSS way to say `background-size: calc(something * height) 100%`. Background-size percentages reference the element's width and height respectively, but you can't cross-reference one axis from the other.

A few options to think through:

1. **Aspect-ratio on background-size with `contain`/`cover`** — won't help here since you want repeat-x with a specific stretch ratio.

2. **Container query units** — `cqh` would give you the container's height, but you're using an inline-size container, so `cqh` isn't available. You could switch to `container-type: size`, but that requires an explicit height on the container, which you probably don't want since the content dictates the height.

3. **`aspect-ratio` on the image itself** — doesn't apply to background images.

4. **A pseudo-element or actual element instead of background** — you could use a `::before` with the image as its background, set to `position: absolute`, and use `aspect-ratio` on the pseudo-element to control its tile dimensions. But that gets complicated with repeating.

5. **SVG with `preserveAspectRatio="none"`** — if you were still using SVG (I know, the bug), you could let the SVG stretch to any size and skip `background-size` entirely.

Honestly, I think the hardcoded width is probably fine. The height of that element is driven by text content, so it's not going to change dramatically. How much does the height actually vary in practice?

content.text:
Fair. Let me rethink.

Option 2 is the most promising path. If you switch to `container-type: size` on `.inner` instead of inline-size on `:host`, you'd get access to `cqh` and could do something like `background-size: calc(cqh * 0.8) 100%`. But that requires `.inner` to have an explicit height, which defeats the purpose since content drives it.

Unless — you keep the inline-size container on `:host` for the layout query, and add a separate `container-type: size` on `.inner` with `height: min-content` or `height: fit-content`. That might give you `cqh` while still letting content drive the height. Worth testing, though `size` containment can be tricky with auto heights.

Want to try that direction?

message.content: Help me build something. On the home page (or maybe elsewhere too) I want to include a component that links to my blog. I'll design it, do the layout, graphics, etc, but I want it to read something like; "I've written 2,346,832 words on design, technology, and life". Where that number is a somewhat accurate count of words from all my blog posts.

content.thinking:
The user wants a word count from all blog posts. This is a build-time computation since the posts are markdown files processed by Eleventy. Let me think about how to approach this.

They need:
1. A way to count all words across all blog posts at build time
2. A component that can display that number

In Eleventy, this could be done a few ways:
- A global data file that computes the count
- A filter or shortcode
- Computed data

Let me look at how data is handled in this project first.

content.text: Let me look at how the site handles data and components to figure out the best approach.

content.text:
Cleanest approach: a global data file that counts words across all posts at build time. Then you can access it anywhere as `{{ blogWordCount }}` or similar.

The `posts` collection uses the `post` tag and each post's `content` is available. But global data files run before collections are built, so a data file can't access collections.

Two good options:

1. **A filter** added in `eleventy.config.js` — something like `totalWordCount` that takes the posts collection and returns a count. You'd call it in your component like `$data.collections.posts | totalWordCount`.

2. **Computed data on the collection** — add a `totalWordCount` property when building the `posts` collection in the config, and stash it somewhere accessible.

Option 1 is simpler and fits the existing patterns. A filter that takes the posts array, strips HTML from each post's content, splits on whitespace, and sums it up. Then format the number however you want in the component.

Want me to go with the filter approach?

message.content: Pick the one that works better. Also, remember each post has a bunch of a frontmatter at the top, that shouldn't be included in the word count. Code blocks also shouldn't be

content.thinking:
The user wants a word count that excludes frontmatter and code blocks. Let me think about this.

In Eleventy, when you access a post's `content` (via `post.content` or `post.templateContent`), the frontmatter has already been stripped — Eleventy parses that out. So frontmatter isn't an issue if we use the rendered content.

However, the rendered content is HTML, so code blocks would be in `<pre><code>` tags. We'd need to strip those out along with all other HTML tags.

Actually wait — stripping all HTML tags would remove the code blocks' content too. 

But we specifically want to exclude code block content while keeping the text content of other HTML elements. So I need to remove `<pre>` blocks first, then strip the remaining HTML tags, and finally count the words. A filter approach works well here since it can process each post's template content. The existing `plainTextPreview` filter already handles stripping HTML tags, so I just need to remove `<pre>` blocks before that step.

content.text: The filter approach is better. In Eleventy, `post.content` is already rendered HTML with frontmatter stripped, so we just need to remove `<pre>` blocks (code) and then count words from the remaining text.

content.text:
That gives you a `totalWordCount` filter. Use it in a template like:

```
$data.collections.posts | totalWordCount
```

It strips `<pre>` blocks (code), then all HTML tags, then counts whitespace-separated words across every post. Frontmatter is already gone by the time Eleventy renders content.

Want to do a quick sanity check on what number it spits out?

<style webc:scoped="home-contents-secondary">
  :host {
    display: flex;
    flex: 1;
    row-gap: var(--g-gutter);
  }

  .home-contents-card {
    flex: 1 1 var(--g-max-3);
    max-width: var(--max-w, var(--g-max-3));
    --card-img-contrast: 1.55;

    & + .home-contents-card {
      margin-left: var(--g-gutter);
    }
  }

  .abstract-container {
    display: flex;
    flex: 1 0 0%;
    overflow: hidden;
    position: relative;

    .graphic {
      background: var(--color-accent-primary);
      position: absolute;
      inset: 0 0 0 var(--g-gutter);
      width: 100%;

      &::after {
        content: "";
        width: 100%;
        height: 100%;
        filter: url(#noise);
        mix-blend-mode: color-dodge;
        position: absolute;
        opacity: 0.7;
        top: 0;
        left: 0;
      }

      video {
        mix-blend-mode: luminosity;
        filter: brightness(1.2);
        object-fit: cover;
        object-position: center;
        height: 101%;
        width: 100%;
      }
    }
  }

  @media (min-width: 1860px) {
    .home-contents-card {
      --max-w: var(--g-max-4);
    }
  }
</style>

message.content:
Help me do something fun. In index.webc I put a placeholder with PUT GRAPHIC HERE. Useing the new images/home/dividier.example.svg, make a component that draws random strokes of different lengths and rotation (like the ones in the example). this should:

- Be internal svg so the strokes can be --color-accent-primary (or currentColor)
- I'd like it to randomize to fit the available width, this can happen on the end of a resize, not on every change of the width
- The height of the element can be set with css. Either rem or vw with a max height

content.thinking:
Looking at the SVG, I can see the pattern. It's a collection of short strokes (lines/paths) at various positions, lengths, and rotations, spread across a wide horizontal area. Each stroke is basically a line segment with a stroke-width of 6.

Let me analyze the strokes to understand the characteristics:
- They're positioned across the full width (0 to ~1597px)
- They have various lengths (short to medium)
- Various angles/rotations
- stroke-width of 6
- The viewBox is 1597 x 32, so it's a very wide, thin strip

For the component I need to:
1. Create an SVG that dynamically fills the available width
2. Generate random strokes using JavaScript to match the pattern
3. Use currentColor for styling flexibility
4. Regenerate the strokes when the container resizes (with debouncing)
5. Control the height through CSS

I'll build a WebC component that measures its width, generates strokes radiating from points along the horizontal axis at random angles and lengths, and listens for resize events to regenerate the pattern as needed.

content.text:
Builds clean. Here's what it does:

- Generates random strokes using `currentColor` (so it picks up `--color-accent-primary` or whatever you set)
- Strokes vary in length (15–75px), angle, and vertical position
- Sometimes clusters 2 strokes together (40% chance)
- Spaces strokes 30–80px apart to fill the width
- Redraws on resize end (150ms debounce)
- Height is `min(3rem, 8vw)` with a `max-height: 4rem` — adjust to taste

content.text:
The HTML is there. The issue is likely that the script uses `document.querySelector(".home-divider")` but by the time it runs, it might not find it, or the element has zero height because it has no content yet and the CSS height isn't applying. Let me check — the scoped class might be the problem. WebC scoping rewrites class names.

Looking at the output: the div has `class="home-divider"` and the CSS uses `.home-divider` — that looks fine actually, since the scoped name matches.

The real issue might be that `home-divider` has `height: min(3rem, 8vw)` but it's inside a flex container with `flex-wrap: wrap`. The div wrapping it might be collapsing. Let me check the wrapper.

message.content: I want to take this same idea and apply it, but in a more controlled way. For the home-contents-card that we use in home-contents-*.webc. For hover and focus-within, I want similar strokes to draw over top of the image in each card. As the user is hovered or focus I want the strokes to redraw(animate).

content.text: The card uses a grid layout with everything stacked in `grid-area: 1 / 1`. I'll add an SVG overlay in the same grid cell, on top of everything, that's hidden by default and draws/animates on hover and focus-within.

content.content: The file /Users/tylergaw/workspace/tylergaw.com/src/_components/home-contents-card.webc has been updated successfully.

content.text:
Here's what this does:

- Adds an SVG overlay in the same grid cell, `z-index: 3` so it sits above the title and image
- Hidden by default (`opacity: 0`), visible on `:hover` and `:focus-within`
- On `mouseenter`/`focusin`: draws strokes immediately and redraws every second
- On `mouseleave`/`focusout`: stops the interval and clears the SVG
- `pointer-events: none` on the SVG so it doesn't interfere with the link click
- Same stroke logic as the divider — bent lines, varied widths, spread across the card dimensions

The SVG has no intrinsic size, so as a grid item it could be collapsing or stretching things. It needs to be positioned absolutely instead of participating in the grid.

file.content:
</style>

<script webc:bucket="components">
  document.querySelectorAll(".home-contents-card").forEach(function (card) {
      const svg = card.querySelector(".strokes");
      if (!svg) return;

      let interval;

      function draw() {
        const w = card.offsetWidth;
        const h = card.offsetHeight;
        svg.setAttribute("viewBox", `0 0 ${w} ${h}`);
        svg.innerHTML = "";

        let x = 5 + Math.random() * 10;

        while (x < w - 5) {
          const len = 15 + Math.random() * 60;
          const angle = (Math.random() - 0.5) * 2.8;
          const dx = Math.cos(angle) * len;
          const dy = Math.sin(angle) * len;
          const edgeBias = Math.random() < 0.5
            ? Math.random() * Math.random() * h * 0.4
            : h - Math.random() * Math.random() * h * 0.4;
          const startY = edgeBias;
          const endX = x + dx;
          const endY = startY + dy;
          const t = 0.3 + Math.random() * 0.4;
          const mx = x + dx * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
          const my = startY + dy * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
          const d = `M${x} ${startY}L${mx} ${my}L${endX} ${endY}`;
          const path = document.createElementNS(
            "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg",
            "path",
          );
          path.setAttribute("d", d);
          path.setAttribute("stroke", "currentColor");
          path.setAttribute("stroke-width", 2 + Math.random() * 4);
          path.setAttribute("stroke-linecap", "butt");
          path.setAttribute("fill", "none");
          svg.appendChild(path);

          x += 5 + Math.random() * 12;
        }
      }

      function start() {
        draw();
        interval = setInterval(draw, 1000);
      }

      function stop() {
        clearInterval(interval);
        svg.innerHTML = "";
      }

      card.addEventListener("mouseenter", start);
      card.addEventListener("mouseleave", stop);
      card.addEventListener("focusin", start);
      card.addEventListener("focusout", stop);
    });
  }
</script>

Is there any way to do this randomized/animated svg strokes without JS and CSS only?

content.text: [Request interrupted by user]

message.content: That's only a question, don't start writing code

content.text:
Not really, no. CSS can't generate DOM elements or randomize values per-element. The closest you could get:

- **CSS `@property` with `@keyframes`** — you can animate custom properties, but you can't use them to create SVG paths or vary the number of elements
- **CSS Houdini `paint()` worklet** — this is actually the closest match. You could write a paint worklet that draws random strokes on a canvas, and it would be pure CSS from the consumer's perspective (`background: paint(strokes)`). But browser support is Chrome/Edge only, no Safari or Firefox
- **A ton of hardcoded elements with `nth-child` randomness** — you could fake randomness with a bunch of `<div>`s and use `nth-child` formulas to vary rotation/position/size. But it's not truly random, it wouldn't adapt to width, and it'd be a mess

The JS approach you have is the right call for what you're doing.

message.content: What's a cool svg filter effect I could do one the strokes in the home-divider? Something subtle maybe. Note I'm using some other grain effects in other places, doesn't have to be that.

content.text:
A few ideas:

- **feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap** — makes the strokes wobble slightly, like they were drawn by hand on rough paper
- **feGaussianBlur at a low value (0.5–1)** — softens the edges just enough that they feel painted rather than vector-crisp
- **feMorphology with operator="erode"** — thins the strokes unevenly, gives a scratchy/worn look
- **feConvolveMatrix** — you can set up a sharpen or emboss kernel that gives the strokes a subtle raised/etched feel

The displacement map one would pair well with what you're already doing — the strokes redraw every second, and if they're also slightly warped, it'd add another layer of movement without being distracting.

message.content: OK, cool. This is an effect I'll want to use more and with maybe more variation, but a similar idea. Knowing that, is there any type of abstraction/DRY you'd apply to make it reusable?

content.text:
The stroke drawing logic is duplicated between `home-divider.webc` and `home-contents-card.webc`. The core — generating random bent paths with displacement filters inside an SVG — is the same. The differences are:

- **Where strokes cluster vertically**: divider uses `midY` with slight variance, cards bias toward edges
- **What triggers drawing**: divider auto-animates on an interval, cards draw on hover/focus
- **Filter presence**: both use the same displacement filter now

I'd extract a single `drawStrokes(svg, options)` function into a shared JS file. Something like:

```
drawStrokes(svg, {
  yBias: "center" | "edges",
  spacing: [5, 12],
  strokeWidth: [2, 4],
  filter: { scale: 3, baseFrequency: 0.04 }
})
```

It handles creating the filter defs, the `<g>`, generating paths, all of it. Then each component just calls that function with its own options and wires up its own triggers (interval, hover, etc.).

<!doctype html>
<html
  lang="en"
  class="no-js"
  style="color-scheme: light"
  :data-page="pageType || page.fileSlug || 'home'"
>
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8" />
    <meta name="theme-color" content="#9A86BF" />
    <meta name="color-scheme" content="light dark" />
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />

    <title
      @text="title || 'Tyler Gaw is a Designer & Engineer in New York City, USA'"
      webc:keep
    ></title>
    <meta
      property="og:title"
      :content="title || 'Tyler Gaw is a Designer & Engineer in New York City, USA'"
    />
    <meta
      name="description"
      :content="meta?.description || 'A personal website. Since 2006'"
    />
    <meta
      property="og:description"
      :content="meta?.description || 'A personal website. Since 2006'"
    />
    <meta
      property="og:image"
      :content="`${site.url}${meta?.image || '/images/social-default.png'}`"
    />
    <meta property="og:url" :content="`${site.url}${page.url}`" />
    <meta property="og:type" content="article" />

    <script webc:keep>
      document.getElementsByTagName("html")[0].classList.replace("no-js", "js");
      // We set any selected color scheme before anything else to avoid any flash.
      const COLOR_SCHEME_STORAGE_KEY = "user-color-scheme";
      document.documentElement.style.colorScheme =
        localStorage.getItem(COLOR_SCHEME_STORAGE_KEY) || "light";
    </script>

    <link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.webmanifest" />
    <link
      rel="alternate"
      :href="`${site.url}/rss.xml`"
      type="application/rss+xml"
      title="An account of the work I do as a designer and engineer"
    />
    <link rel="icon" href="/images/favicon.svg" type="image/svg+xml" />
    <link rel="icon" href="/images/favicon.png" type="image/png" />
    <link
      rel="preload"
      href="/fonts/protogroteskweb-light.woff2"
      as="font"
      type="font/woff2"
      crossorigin
    />
    <link
      rel="preload"
      href="/fonts/protogroteskweb-bold.woff2"
      as="font"
      type="font/woff2"
      crossorigin
    />
    <link rel="me" href="https://mastodon.social/@tylergaw" />

    <style webc:keep>
      @view-transition {
        navigation: auto;
      }
      ::view-transition-old(root),
      ::view-transition-new(root),
      ::view-transition-group(root) {
        animation-duration: 0s;
      }
    </style>

    <link rel="stylesheet" href="/css/tylergaw.css" webc:keep />
    <style @raw="getBundle('css')" webc:keep></style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <page-header></page-header>
    <main @raw="content"></main>
    <page-footer :@year="2026"></page-footer>

    <script @raw="getBundle('js', 'components')" webc:keep></script>
  </body>
</html>

<script webc:bucket="components">
  function drawStrokes(svg, opts) {
    opts = opts || {};
    const container = svg.parentElement;
    const w = container.offsetWidth;
    const h = container.offsetHeight;
    const filterId = opts.filterId || "strokes-warp";

    svg.setAttribute("viewBox", `0 0 ${w} ${h}`);
    svg.innerHTML = "";

    const defs = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "defs");
    defs.innerHTML = `<filter id="${filterId}">
      <feTurbulence type="turbulence" baseFrequency="0.04" numOctaves="2" result="turbulence" seed="${Math.floor(Math.random() * 100)}" />
      <feDisplacementMap in="SourceGraphic" in2="turbulence" scale="3" xChannelSelector="R" yChannelSelector="G" />
    </filter>`;
    svg.appendChild(defs);

    const g = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "g");
    g.setAttribute("filter", `url(#${filterId})`);
    svg.appendChild(g);

    const margin = opts.margin || 5;
    let x = margin + Math.random() * (opts.startSpread || 10);

    while (x < w - margin) {
      const len = 15 + Math.random() * 60;
      const angle = (Math.random() - 0.5) * 2.8;
      const dx = Math.cos(angle) * len;
      const dy = Math.sin(angle) * len;

      let startY;
      if (opts.yBias === "edges") {
        startY = Math.random() < 0.5
          ? Math.random() * Math.random() * h * 0.4
          : h - Math.random() * Math.random() * h * 0.4;
      } else {
        const midY = h / 2;
        startY = midY + (Math.random() - 0.5) * h * 0.4;
      }

      const endX = x + dx;
      const endY = startY + dy;
      const t = 0.3 + Math.random() * 0.4;
      const mx = x + dx * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
      const my = startY + dy * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
      const d = `M${x} ${startY}L${mx} ${my}L${endX} ${endY}`;

      const path = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "path");
      path.setAttribute("d", d);
      path.setAttribute("stroke", "currentColor");
      path.setAttribute("stroke-width", 2 + Math.random() * 4);
      path.setAttribute("stroke-linecap", "butt");
      path.setAttribute("fill", "none");
      g.appendChild(path);

      x += 5 + Math.random() * 12;
    }
  }
</script>

    function draw() {
      const w = el.offsetWidth;
      const h = el.offsetHeight;

      svg.setAttribute("viewBox", `0 0 ${w} ${h}`);
      svg.innerHTML = "";

      const defs = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "defs");
      defs.innerHTML = `<filter id="divider-warp">
        <feTurbulence type="turbulence" baseFrequency="0.04" numOctaves="2" result="turbulence" seed="${Math.floor(Math.random() * 100)}" />
        <feDisplacementMap in="SourceGraphic" in2="turbulence" scale="3" xChannelSelector="R" yChannelSelector="G" />
      </filter>`;
      svg.appendChild(defs);

      const g = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "g");
      g.setAttribute("filter", "url(#divider-warp)");
      svg.appendChild(g);

      const midY = h / 2;
      let x = 10 + Math.random() * 20;

      while (x < w - 10) {
        const len = 15 + Math.random() * 60;
        const angle = (Math.random() - 0.5) * 2.8;
        const dx = Math.cos(angle) * len;
        const dy = Math.sin(angle) * len;
        const startY = midY + (Math.random() - 0.5) * h * 0.4;
        const endX = x + dx;
        const endY = startY + dy;
        const bent = Math.random() < 1;
        let d;
        if (bent) {
          const t = 0.3 + Math.random() * 0.4;
          const mx = x + dx * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
          const my = startY + dy * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
          d = `M${x} ${startY}L${mx} ${my}L${endX} ${endY}`;
        } else {
          d = `M${x} ${startY}L${endX} ${endY}`;
        }
        const path = document.createElementNS(
          "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg",
          "path",
        );
        path.setAttribute("d", d);
        path.setAttribute("stroke", "currentColor");
        path.setAttribute("stroke-width", 2 + Math.random() * 4);
        path.setAttribute("stroke-linecap", "butt");
        path.setAttribute("fill", "none");
        g.appendChild(path);

        x += 5 + Math.random() * 12;
      }
    }

    let resizeTimer;
    const intervalDuration = 1000;
    let interval = setInterval(draw, intervalDuration);

    window.addEventListener("resize", function () {
      clearTimeout(resizeTimer);
      clearInterval(interval);
      resizeTimer = setTimeout(function () {
        draw();
        interval = setInterval(draw, intervalDuration);
      }, 150);
    });

    draw();
  })();
</script>

input.old_string:
<script webc:bucket="components">
  document.querySelectorAll(".home-contents-card").forEach(function (card, i) {
    const svg = card.querySelector(".strokes");
    if (!svg) return;

    const filterId = "card-warp-" + i;
    let interval;

    function draw() {
      const w = card.offsetWidth;
      const h = card.offsetHeight;
      svg.setAttribute("viewBox", `0 0 ${w} ${h}`);
      svg.innerHTML = "";

      const defs = document.createElementNS(
        "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg",
        "defs",
      );
      defs.innerHTML = `<filter id="${filterId}">
        <feTurbulence type="turbulence" baseFrequency="0.04" numOctaves="2" result="turbulence" seed="${Math.floor(Math.random() * 100)}" />
        <feDisplacementMap in="SourceGraphic" in2="turbulence" scale="3" xChannelSelector="R" yChannelSelector="G" />
      </filter>`;
      svg.appendChild(defs);

      const g = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "g");
      g.setAttribute("filter", `url(#${filterId})`);
      svg.appendChild(g);

      let x = 5 + Math.random() * 10;

      while (x < w - 5) {
        const len = 15 + Math.random() * 60;
        const angle = (Math.random() - 0.5) * 2.8;
        const dx = Math.cos(angle) * len;
        const dy = Math.sin(angle) * len;
        const edgeBias =
          Math.random() < 0.5
            ? Math.random() * Math.random() * h * 0.4
            : h - Math.random() * Math.random() * h * 0.4;
        const startY = edgeBias;
        const endX = x + dx;
        const endY = startY + dy;
        const t = 0.3 + Math.random() * 0.4;
        const mx = x + dx * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
        const my = startY + dy * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
        const d = `M${x} ${startY}L${mx} ${my}L${endX} ${endY}`;
        const path = document.createElementNS(
          "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg",
          "path",
        );
        path.setAttribute("d", d);
        path.setAttribute("stroke", "currentColor");
        path.setAttribute("stroke-width", 2 + Math.random() * 4);
        path.setAttribute("stroke-linecap", "butt");
        path.setAttribute("fill", "none");
        g.appendChild(path);

        x += 5 + Math.random() * 12;
      }
    }

    function start() {
      draw();
      interval = setInterval(draw, 500);
    }

    function stop() {
      clearInterval(interval);
      svg.innerHTML = "";
    }

    card.addEventListener("mouseenter", start);
    card.addEventListener("mouseleave", stop);
    card.addEventListener("focusin", start);
    card.addEventListener("focusout", stop);
  });
</script>

content.text:
Done. Here's what changed:

- **`draw-strokes.webc`** — New component with the shared `drawStrokes(svg, opts)` function in `webc:bucket="components"`. Options: `filterId`, `yBias` ("center" default or "edges"), `margin`, `startSpread`.
- **`layout-main.webc`** — Added `<draw-strokes>` so the function is bundled on every page.
- **`home-divider.webc`** — Switched from `webc:keep` to `webc:bucket="components"`, replaced inline stroke logic with `drawStrokes(svg, { filterId: "divider-warp", margin: 10, startSpread: 20 })`.
- **`home-contents-card.webc`** — Replaced inline stroke logic with `drawStrokes(svg, { filterId: "card-warp-" + i, yBias: "edges" })`.

#!/bin/bash
set -e

REPORT="./perf-reports/$(date +%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S)"
PORT=9999

pnpm build
mkdir -p perf-reports

npx -y serve dist -l $PORT &
SERVER_PID=$!
sleep 2

npx -y lighthouse http://localhost:$PORT --preset=desktop --output=html --output-path="$REPORT.html"
open -a "Google Chrome" "$REPORT.html"

kill $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true

content.text: Here's the Lighthouse results summary: ### Scores | Category | Score | |---|---| | Performance | **97** | | Accessibility | **95** | | Best Practices | **100** | | SEO | **100** | ### Core Web Vitals | Metric | Value | Score | |---|---|---| | FCP | 0.7s | 98 | | LCP | 1.0s | 95 | | Speed Index | 1.4s | 86 | | TBT | 0ms | 100 | | CLS | 0 | 100 | | TTI | 1.0s | 100 | ### Issues to flag 1. **Color contrast (accessibility fail)** — Multiple elements are flagging for insufficient contrast. The main culprits: - `p.timeline` in the page header - The `.invocation` text in the abstract container - The `home-contents-tertiary` `.card` links (text on `--color-accent-primary` background) - Footer nav links These are all likely cases where Lighthouse can't resolve CSS custom properties or `light-dark()` values, so it may be a false positive. Worth spot-checking manually against the actual rendered colors. 2. **Speed Index at 86** — The only sub-90 performance metric. At 1.4s it's still solid, but this is likely driven by the progressive rendering of the hero area and video content. Not something I'd worry about unless it regresses. 3. **LCP request discovery scored 0** — This is an informational/diagnostic audit, not a scoring one. It flags when the LCP resource isn't discoverable in the initial HTML. Since your LCP element is likely a `` or `` inside a WebC component, the resource is in the markup and should be fine in practice. No actionable performance regressions from the `drawStrokes` refactor — the scores look clean.

  --prod-if-unlockedDeploy to production if unlocked, create a draft otherwise

  --debugPrint debugging information

  -p, --prodDeploy to production (default: false)
  -s, --site <name-or-id>A project name or ID to deploy to
  --site-name <name>Name for a new site. Implies --create-site if the site does

       of the deployment (default: false)
  --team <slug>Specify team slug when creating a site. Only works with

  --triggerTrigger a new build of your project on Netlify without uploading
       local files

DESCRIPTION
  
  Builds and deploys your project to Netlify. Creates a draft deploy by default.
  Use --prod to deploy directly to your live site.
  
  The deploy command will:
  - Build your project (unless --no-build is specified)
  - Upload static files, functions, and edge functions
  - Process redirects and headers from netlify.toml or _redirects/_headers files
  - Provide deploy and function logs URLs
  
  For detailed configuration options, see the Netlify documentation.

EXAMPLES
  $ netlify deploy
  $ netlify deploy --site my-first-project
  $ netlify deploy --no-build # Deploy without running a build first
  $ netlify deploy --prod
  $ netlify deploy --prod --open
  $ netlify deploy --prod-if-unlocked
  $ netlify deploy --message "A message with an $ENV_VAR"
  $ netlify deploy --auth $NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN
  $ netlify deploy --trigger
  $ netlify deploy --context deploy-preview
  $ netlify deploy --site-name my-new-site --team my-team # Create site and deploy
  $ netlify deploy --allow-anonymous --dir ./public --no-build # Deploy without auth

# See https://docs.netlify.com/configure-builds/file-based-configuration
# for available configuration.
[build]
# Default build command.
command = "npm build"

# Directory that contains the deploy-ready HTML files and assets generated by
# the build. This is relative to the base directory if one has been set, or the
# root directory if a base has not been set. This sample publishes the
# directory located at the absolute path "root/project/build-output"
publish = "dist/"

# This should go away when/if I update to a more recent version of eleventy
# netlify dev, by default, just runs `eleventy`, which is not what we want.
[dev]
command = "eleventy --serve"

[[redirects]]
from = "/articles"
to = "/blog"
status = 301
force = true

[[redirects]]
from = "/articles/*"
to = "/blog/:splat"
status = 301
force = true

[[redirects]]
from = "/library"
to = "/bookshelf"
status = 301
force = true

file.content:
# See https://docs.netlify.com/configure-builds/file-based-configuration
# for available configuration.
[build]
# Default build command.
command = "npm build"

# Directory that contains the deploy-ready HTML files and assets generated by
# the build. This is relative to the base directory if one has been set, or the
# root directory if a base has not been set. This sample publishes the
# directory located at the absolute path "root/project/build-output"
publish = "dist/"

content.thinking:
Wait, but the user said the build failed on this PR. But the previous deploys on main succeeded with the same `netlify.toml`. Let me check - the previous deploys show "ready" for main. So `npm build` was working before. Maybe Netlify updated their build image and now it uses a newer npm version that doesn't support `npm build` as a shorthand?

Actually, `npm build` is a real npm command (it's a deprecated lifecycle script runner), but it's NOT the same as `npm run build`. In older npm versions, `npm build` might have triggered the build script or done something else. 

In newer npm versions, `npm build` isn't recognized as a valid command anymore. The recent successful deploys on main suggest the build image might have just been updated, or there's a version mismatch happening. I should check if there's a `.node-version` or `.nvmrc` file that specifies which Node version to use, or if `netlify.toml` has a Node version configured.

The real issue is that `npm build` doesn't work locally with the Netlify CLI either, but it probably worked on their older build image. Now that they've updated, the fix is straightforward: switch `npm build` to `npm run build` and test it.

Now back to the Netlify build failure — the error is "dependency_installation script returned non-zero exit code: 1". The `npm run build` fix was correct but the build is failing earlier, during dependency install. Let me check what's going on.

content.text:
There's a `pnpm-lock.yaml` but no `packageManager` field in `package.json`. Netlify sees the lockfile and tries to use pnpm, but likely doesn't have the right version installed or corepack isn't set up. And the build command uses `npm run build`.

Let me check what the last successful deploy on main used — the previous deploys on main were "ready", so something changed. Let me check the git diff between main and this branch for anything that could affect dependency install.

content.text:
Now I see it. On main, `netlify.toml` had `command = "yarn build"` and there was likely a `yarn.lock`. This branch switched to pnpm with a `pnpm-lock.yaml` but removed `yarn.lock`. The build command was changed to `npm build` (wrong). Plus the lockfile change means Netlify's dependency detection changed.

Let me see what lockfile main has.

input.command: git ls-tree main -- pnpm-lock.yaml package-lock.json yarn.lock 2>&1
input.description: Check which lockfiles exist on main

content.text:
Main has `yarn.lock`. This branch has `pnpm-lock.yaml` instead. Netlify detects pnpm from the lockfile and tries to install with pnpm, but it's probably not available or configured on their build image.

The simplest fix: tell Netlify to use npm for dependency installation by removing the pnpm lockfile and generating an npm one, OR keep pnpm and configure Netlify to use it.

Since the project locally uses pnpm and the package.json scripts use plain commands (no pnpm prefix), the cleanest approach is to just use npm on Netlify. I need to generate a `package-lock.json` so Netlify uses npm, and remove the pnpm lockfile from git tracking (you can keep it locally).

Actually, let me think about this differently. What does the user actually use? They have `pnpm-lock.yaml` locally. But Netlify needs to know what to do. The easiest fix might be to just add `package-lock.json` or tell Netlify's environment to use pnpm.

Let me just generate a `package-lock.json` and add it alongside. Or better — configure Netlify to use pnpm since that's what the project uses.

input.description: Check local node and pnpm versions

content.text: I'll configure Netlify to use pnpm by setting the `PNPM_FLAGS` and node version env vars in `netlify.toml`, and also ensure the build command uses pnpm.

# Directory that contains the deploy-ready HTML files and assets generated by

content.text:
Now let me also add `corepack enable` so Netlify can use pnpm. Netlify's build image should support corepack, but we need the `packageManager` field in package.json to make it work reliably. Actually, Netlify auto-detects pnpm from `pnpm-lock.yaml` and installs it — the issue is likely the node version. Netlify's default node version might be too old.

Let me test with the Netlify CLI.

content.text:
Can't get logs from the API. The error is "Build script returned non-zero exit code: 2" — exit code 2 from pnpm typically means a missing script or command not found. The issue is likely that Netlify's node 22 doesn't have `pnpm` available, or the `ELEVENTY_ENV=production` syntax doesn't work in their shell.

Let me check if `pnpm build` works properly — the `build` script sets `ELEVENTY_ENV=production eleventy`. On Netlify, environment vars can be set differently. Let me also check if `ELEVENTY_ENV` can be set in the netlify.toml instead.

input.prompt: Find the API endpoint for retrieving build logs or deploy logs. I need the path and method for fetching the log output of a deploy or build. Look for anything with "log" in the path.

content.content:
# Netlify API Log Endpoints

Based on the documentation provided, I found **one relevant endpoint**:

**Build Log Message**
- **Path:** `POST /sites/{site_id}/builds/{build_id}/log`
- **Operation:** `postupdateSiteBuildLog`
- **Purpose:** "Update site build log"
- **Authentication:** Requires `_netlifyAuth_`

This endpoint allows you to submit log messages during a build process. However, the documentation doesn't explicitly show a dedicated GET endpoint for retrieving complete deploy or build log output. The "Build log message" operation appears to be for *writing* to logs rather than reading them.

For comprehensive log retrieval, you may need to consult Netlify's complete API documentation or community forums, as the provided excerpt doesn't detail a specific log *retrieval* endpoint.

content.text:
The Netlify REST API doesn't expose build log output — it only has the error summary. The logs are only available in the Netlify dashboard UI (which requires JavaScript to render). 

There's another option — Netlify has an MCP server that might give us access. Or we could set up the Netlify CLI to stream logs on future deploys. But for existing deploys, I don't think there's a programmatic way to pull the full build output.

Two paths forward:

1. **You paste the build log from the Netlify dashboard** so I can see what's actually failing, instead of me guessing.
2. **We set up `netlify-cli watch`** or `netlify logs --source deploy --follow` to stream logs for the next deploy in real time, so I can see future failures directly.

Which do you prefer? Or if you know of another way to get at those logs, I'm all ears.

## Netlify MCP Server for Claude Code

The Netlify MCP Server includes tools to check deployment status and build logs, manage environment variables, and list serverless functions. This allows you to deploy, manage, and configure Netlify sites, builds, and serverless functions directly from Claude Code.

## Setting Up Access

To access Netlify logs through Claude Code, you can use the Netlify MCP Server plugin. If you run into authentication issues, you can add a Netlify Personal Access Token (PAT) to your MCP configuration. To create a token, select your user icon in the Netlify dashboard, go to User settings > OAuth > New access token, copy your token, and add it to your MCP config.

## Claude Web and Desktop Connectors

Additionally, if you're using Claude Web or Claude Desktop, you can use the Netlify connector to create, deploy, and manage websites on Netlify, controlling all aspects of your site from creating secrets to enforcing access controls.

input.prompt: Extract the exact installation instructions for the Netlify Claude Code plugin. How do I install it? What's the command or configuration needed?

content.content:
# Netlify Claude Code Plugin Installation

## Installation Command

The quickest way to install the Netlify plugin is to run:

```
/plugin install netlify
```

## How It Works

According to the documentation, once you execute this command in Claude Code, "The plugin will be enabled automatically" and you can immediately start using the plugin's features in your Claude Code sessions.

## What You Get

After installation, you'll have access to four main commands:

- **netlify:deploy** — Deploy current project to Netlify
- **netlify:status** — Check deployment status and build logs
- **netlify:env** — Manage environment variables for the site
- **netlify:functions** — List and manage serverless functions

The plugin costs $0 and works cross-platform through the TypeScript-based Netlify MCP (Model Context Protocol) server.

input.prompt: Extract the complete setup instructions for adding the Netlify MCP server to Claude Code. I need the exact MCP configuration, commands, env vars, and any authentication steps.

content.content:
# Claude Code Netlify MCP Server Setup

## Recommended Setup (Remote MCP Server)

Run this command in your terminal:

```bash
claude mcp add --transport http netlify https://netlify-mcp.netlify.app/mcp
```

This connects Claude Code to the remote Netlify MCP server.

## Alternative: Local Setup

If remote MCP servers are blocked in your environment, run:

```bash
claude mcp add netlify -- npx -y @netlify/mcp
```

This installs and runs the Netlify MCP server locally.

## Authentication

No explicit environment variables are specified in the documentation. Authentication occurs through:

1. **Netlify CLI**: The MCP server uses your existing Netlify CLI authentication
2. **Login Prompt**: Claude Code can prompt you to log in if needed
3. **Manual Login**: Run `netlify login` in your terminal if required

For CLI authentication details, refer to the [CLI auth documentation](/api-and-cli-guides/cli-guides/get-started-with-cli/#authentication).

## Quick Context Setup (Alternative Approach)

If you prefer not to set up MCP separately, you can instruct Claude Code:

```
fetch https://netlify.ai to help me deploy and build with Netlify using the latest agent skills
```

## Agent Skills Installation

Additionally, install Netlify agent skills:

```bash
npx skills add netlify/context-and-tools --skill '*' --yes
```

## Resources

- **MCP Server Repository**: [netlify-mcp on GitHub](https://github.com/netlify/context-and-tools)
- **Agent Skills Repository**: [netlify/context-and-tools](https://github.com/netlify/context-and-tools)
- **Claude Code MCP Docs**: [code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp)

input.prompt: Extract the COMPLETE authentication instructions for the Netlify MCP server with Claude Code. Include every step, every command, every detail about tokens, OAuth, environment variables, or any other auth mechanism needed.

content.content:
# Authentication Instructions for Netlify MCP Server with Claude Code

Based on the documentation provided, here are the complete authentication instructions:

## Quick Setup

1. **Add the Netlify MCP Server** to Claude Code:
```bash
claude mcp add --transport http netlify https://netlify-mcp.netlify.app/mcp
```

Or for local setup (if remote MCP servers are blocked):
```bash
claude mcp add netlify -- npx -y @netlify/mcp
```

2. **Authenticate to Netlify** via CLI when needed:
```bash
netlify login
```

## Authentication Flow

The documentation indicates that:

- **No upfront authentication required** - You can start using Claude Code without pre-existing Netlify credentials
- **On-demand login** - Claude Code will prompt you to log in when needed (e.g., when deploying)
- **Token-based** - The CLI uses personal access tokens for authentication

## Key Authentication Notes

### CLI Authentication
- Use `netlify login` to authenticate via the command line
- For detailed CLI auth instructions, see the [CLI authentication docs](/api-and-cli-guides/cli-guides/get-started-with-cli/#authentication)

### Security Best Practices
- **Never commit** Netlify CLI config files or personal access tokens to your repository
- If your team uses SSO, ensure your token is authorized for the team
- Store tokens securely outside your repo

### Troubleshooting Authentication
- If your company has enabled Netlify SSO, you may need to use a different work login or personal email
- You can test connectivity with: `netlify status`
- Verify the correct account with: `netlify login`

## Alternative: Anonymous Deploys

For testing without authentication:
```bash
netlify deploy --allow-anonymous
```
- Generates a temporary live URL
- Expires within 1 hour unless claimed
- Requires logging in or creating a Netlify account to claim ownership

input.prompt: Find the authentication setup instructions for the Netlify MCP server. How does auth work? Is there an OAuth callback? Do you need to set NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN? How do you authenticate the remote HTTP MCP server specifically?

content.content:
# Netlify MCP Server Authentication

Based on the GitHub repository content, here's what's documented about the Netlify MCP server authentication:

**OAuth Flow:**
The setup uses OAuth for authorization. According to the README: "authorizes via OAuth on first use — no token or local install required."

**HTTP Connection:**
The server operates remotely over HTTP at `https://netlify-mcp.netlify.app/mcp` and doesn't require pre-configured credentials for Claude Code and Grok Build plugins, which bundle the server automatically.

**For Other Clients:**
The documentation notes that "Cursor, Codex, Copilot don't bundle the MCP server — add it to those clients manually using the Netlify MCP docs."

**No Local Token Required:**
Unlike traditional setups, this approach eliminates the need to set `NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN` environment variables locally since OAuth handles authentication on first use.

For detailed setup instructions beyond these fundamentals, you'd need to consult the [official Netlify MCP documentation](https://docs.netlify.com/build/build-with-ai/netlify-mcp-server/), which isn't fully included in this repository README.

input.description: Check MCP config for this specific project

content.text:
The config looks correct. The server is there but shows "Needs authentication" which means the OAuth flow needs to happen interactively. Since it's an HTTP MCP server with OAuth, it should open a browser window to authorize when you first interact with it.

You likely need to restart Claude Code so it attempts the connection fresh, and when it does, it should pop open a browser window for the Netlify OAuth. The `! Needs authentication` status in `claude mcp list` is the server telling you to complete that flow. 

Try restarting the session — when it starts up and tries to connect the MCP server, it should prompt you in the browser to authorize.

input.prompt: Give me the COMPLETE page content. Every single instruction, every command, every note. I need to see everything including how authentication works for the remote HTTP MCP server specifically. Don't summarize — give me everything.

content.content:
# Complete Page Content: Set up Claude Code for Netlify

## Page Overview
This is the "Set up Claude Code for Netlify" documentation page from Netlify Docs, located at `/build/build-with-ai/agent-setup-guides/set-up-claude-code-for-netlify/`.

## Main Content Sections

### Prerequisites (Before you begin)
- Claude Code installed
- Node 24 or higher installed
- Recommended: Have a project ready for testing deployment
- Optional: Netlify personal account or team workspace (Claude Code can prompt to create one when needed)

### Quick Setup with `netlify.ai`
**Command:**
```
fetch https://netlify.ai to help me deploy and build with Netlify using the latest agent skills
```
This equips the agent with the latest Netlify context including agent skills.

### Connect to the Netlify MCP Server

#### Recommended Remote Setup
**Command:**
```
claude mcp add --transport http netlify https://netlify-mcp.netlify.app/mcp
```

**Key Details:**
- Uses HTTP transport (remote server)
- Server URL: `https://netlify-mcp.netlify.app/mcp`
- This is the recommended approach for most users
- Learn more: [Netlify's MCP server](https://github.com/netlify/netlify-mcp) and [MCP setup with Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp)

#### Local Alternative (if remote blocked)
**Command:**
```
claude mcp add netlify -- npx -y @netlify/mcp
```
- Used when remote MCP servers are blocked in your environment
- Runs locally using the `@netlify/mcp` package

### Install Agent Skills for Netlify
**Command:**
```
npx skills add netlify/context-and-tools --skill '*' --yes
```

**Details:**
- Installs all agent skills (`'*'` flag)
- Source: [Official agent skills repository](https://github.com/netlify/context-and-tools)
- Can be invoked directly in Claude Code using `/<skill-name>` syntax
- Helps agents build with Netlify best practices

### Authentication

#### CLI Authentication
- Use `netlify login` to authenticate
- For SSO-enabled companies: May need to use different work login or personal email
- Reference: [CLI auth documentation](/api-and-cli-guides/cli-guides/get-started-with-cli/#authentication)

#### Credentials Management
- **Never commit** Netlify CLI config files or personal access tokens to repo
- For SSO teams: Ensure token is authorized for the team workspace
- Learn more: [Setting up Netlify access token](/api-and-cli-guides/cli-guides/get-started-with-cli/#authentication)

### Deployment Options

| Method | Use Case | Command |
|--------|----------|---------|
| Draft Deploy | Safe test before production | `netlify deploy` |
| Production Deploy | Deploy to live version; first deploy or updates | `netlify deploy --prod` |
| Anonymous Deploy | Temporary testing/AI agent flows; expires in 1 hour unless claimed | `netlify deploy --allow-anonymous` |

### Building with Primitives (Prompt Examples)
After confirming successful deployment, try:
- "Add a new contact page to my site using Netlify Forms with spam protection"
- "Convert this Express endpoint into a Netlify Function"
- "Implement an Edge Function that geolocates users and rewrites to localized content"
- "Check my netlify.toml configuration for issues"

## Troubleshooting Sections

### Authentication Issues
- Check if company has Netlify SSO enabled
- Try different login credentials (work vs personal)
- Use `netlify login` command
- For non-sensitive projects: Try anonymous deploy

### Deploy Issues
- Use `netlify deploy --allow-anonymous` to isolate auth issues
- Only recommended for non-sensitive projects (expires in 1 hour)

### Netlify CLI Issues
**Verification commands:**
```
netlify status          # Check if installed
netlify login          # Test connection to correct account
netlify --help         # Verify setup
```

**Installation:**
```
npm install -g netlify-cli
```

### Netlify Skills Troubleshooting
**Reinstall/update:**
```
npx -y skills add netlify/context-and-tools --skill '*' --yes --agent claude-code
```

- Reference skills directly from [Netlify agent skills repository](https://github.com/netlify/context-and-tools)
- Invoke with `/<skill-name>` syntax in Claude Code
- Reference skill file URLs directly from repository

### Node Version Issues
- Recommended: Node 24 or higher
- Check: `node --version`
- With nvm: `nvm install 24` and `nvm use 24`

## Technical Implementation Details

### MCP Server Configuration (Remote HTTP)
The page mentions the remote MCP server but does NOT provide explicit authentication details. Based on the setup:
- **Transport:** HTTP
- **URL:** `https://netlify-mcp.netlify.app/mcp`
- **Authentication:** Implied to use Netlify CLI authentication tokens (via `netlify login`)
- The MCP connection likely reuses credentials established via `netlify login`

The documentation defers to:
- [Netlify's MCP server GitHub](https://github.com/netlify/context-and-tools)
- [Claude Code MCP documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp)

For detailed authentication flow, see [CLI auth docs](/api-and-cli-guides/cli-guides/get-started-with-cli/#authentication)

## Page Metadata
- **Last Updated:** Jul 8, 2026
- **Previous Page:** Overview
- **Next Page:** Codex setup guide
- **Breadcrumb:** Build / Build With AI / Agent Setup Guides

- Reference skills directly from [Netlify agent skills repository](https://github.com/netlify/context-and-tools)
- Invoke with `/<skill-name>` syntax in Claude Code
- Reference skill file URLs directly from repository

### Node Version Issues
- Recommended: Node 24 or higher
- Check: `node --version`
- With nvm: `nvm install 24` and `nvm use 24`

## Technical Implementation Details

### MCP Server Configuration (Remote HTTP)
The page mentions the remote MCP server but does NOT provide explicit authentication details. Based on the setup:
- **Transport:** HTTP
- **URL:** `https://netlify-mcp.netlify.app/mcp`
- **Authentication:** Implied to use Netlify CLI authentication tokens (via `netlify login`)
- The MCP connection likely reuses credentials established via `netlify login`

The documentation defers to:
- [Netlify's MCP server GitHub](https://github.com/netlify/context-and-tools)
- [Claude Code MCP documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp)

For detailed authentication flow, see [CLI auth docs](/api-and-cli-guides/cli-guides/get-started-with-cli/#authentication)

## Page Metadata
- **Last Updated:** Jul 8, 2026
- **Previous Page:** Overview
- **Next Page:** Codex setup guide
- **Breadcrumb:** Build / Build With AI / Agent Setup Guides

content.text:
npm install works fine. The issue must be in the build command itself. On Netlify, the command runs in the project root after deps are installed. Exit code 2 from `npx eleventy`... Let me check — does Netlify have npx available? On their build image with Node 22, it should. But maybe eleventy isn't in the path after an npm install.

Actually wait — I bet the problem is simpler. Netlify detects `pnpm-lock.yaml` and tries to install with pnpm, but pnpm might not be available or might fail silently, and then `npx eleventy` can't find the package. The first deploy (`npm build`) failed at dependency install. The second (`pnpm build`) also failed at dependency install. The third (`npx eleventy`) got past install but failed at build — so deps installed but maybe incorrectly.

Can you paste the build log from the dashboard? That'll end this guessing immediately.

content.text:
Main uses `yarn build` with a `yarn.lock`. This branch deleted `yarn.lock` and has `pnpm-lock.yaml` instead. Netlify sees the pnpm lockfile, tries to use pnpm, and things break.

The simplest fix: generate a `yarn.lock` so Netlify can use yarn like it always did, and set the build command back to `yarn build`.

content.text: Back to "dependency_installation script returned non-zero exit code: 1". Now there's both a `yarn.lock` and `pnpm-lock.yaml` — Netlify is probably confused by having two lockfiles.

input.command:
git rm pnpm-lock.yaml && git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Removes pnpm-lock.yaml, keeps yarn.lock for Netlify

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push
input.description: Remove conflicting pnpm lockfile, commit and push
